time_period: Value creation plan with a four-year time horizon
body:
• Announces a value creation plan to "align executive leadership rewards directly with the delivery of sustained shareholder value".
  • Updates the executive board's long-term incentive arrangement via a one-off award "designed to incentivise the pursuit of strategic growth opportunities".
  • Plan design links (and increases) executive compensation to share price milestone achievement.
  • Executive board "value creation pool" rises to GBP12.3 million if the share price reaches GBP2.75.
  • Executive board "value creation pool" rises to GBP20.5 million if the share price reaches GBP3.0.
  • The GBP20.5 million pool is GBP8.2 million higher than the GBP12.3 million pool (+66.7%).
  • CEO Stephnie Judge receives 25% of the overall incentive pool regardless of share price (implies GBP3.075 million at the GBP12.3m pool; GBP5.125 million at the GBP20.5m pool).
  • CFO Daniel Barton receives 20% of the overall incentive pool regardless of share price (implies GBP2.46 million at the GBP12.3m pool; GBP4.10 million at the GBP20.5m pool).
  • Implied remaining allocation to other executive board members is 55% of the overall pool.

time_period: Order delivery through May 2028; revenue recognition from H2 2026
body:
• Awarded $63.5 million of orders for its titanium stress joint (TSJ) product line for a new offshore development in Guyana.
  • Award to be completed by the Group's Subsea Spring business unit.
  • Deliveries scheduled through to May 2028.
  • Revenue expected to be recognised from H2 2026.
  • New TSJ order is in addition to $4.4 million of orders already secured for the same development since December 2025 (via Stafford and Flexible Engineered Solutions).
  • Total orders disclosed for this development since December 2025 are $67.9 million ($63.5m + $4.4m).
  • The $63.5m TSJ order is $59.1m higher than the $4.4m previously disclosed (+1,343%, or ~14.4x).
  • Company signals "further incremental orders likely to be received throughout the rest of 2026."
  • Positions TSJ adoption on FPSO projects as supporting a strategy to capture more subsea spend by leveraging an enlarged product offering across the well life cycle.
  • CEO Jim Johnson said: "Our TSJ product line, which is a critical component for offshore developments, continues to be adopted on key offshore projects utilising FPSOs, given the maintenance benefits and reliability offered. We would like to thank our partner in Guyana for its continued confidence in our product offering and look forward to working together in the future with our range of products and solutions. This order also contributes to our guided subsea product group revenue and EBITDA through to 2028, as published at our subsea investor event held in January 2026."

time_period: Modified Dutch Auction tender offer expired April 1, 2026
body:
• Announced final results of its "modified Dutch Auction" tender offer, which expired at one minute after 11:59 P.M., New York City time, on April 1, 2026.
  • Deposit count (Equiniti Trust Company, LLC): 17,577,250 ordinary shares were properly tendered and not properly withdrawn at or below $92.00 per share.
  • Notes that not all shares tendered via notice of guaranteed delivery were delivered within the required settlement period.
  • Accepted for payment 17,577,250 shares (all properly tendered and not properly withdrawn) at $92.00 per share.
  • Aggregate repurchase cost stated as ~$1.617 billion, excluding fees and expenses.
  • Repurchased shares represent ~29.7% of shares outstanding as of April 1, 2026.
  • Implied total shares outstanding as of April 1, 2026 are ~59.2 million (based on 17.577m being 29.7%).
  • Nir Zohar, President and Co-founder, stated: "I am very pleased with the outcome of our Dutch auction tender offer, which is enabling us to repurchase nearly 30% of our market capitalization and return meaningful value to investors."

time_period: Event announcement (Q1 2026 results scheduled for 23 April 2026)
body:
• Q1 2026 financial results will be released after market close on Thursday, 23 April 2026.
  • Live audio webcast scheduled for 23 April 2026 at 5:30 p.m. ET, with presentation slides available online.
  • Earnings release and webcast replay will be posted on the company's investor site.

time_period: Takeover approach process update (8 April 2026; deadline extended to 6 May 2026)
body:
• Extended the UK Takeover Code "put up or shut up" deadline for Alamadiyaf al-Masiyyah for Trading LLC to 6 May 2026 (from 8 April 2026).
  • Stated it has received multiple unsolicited takeover proposals.
  • Extension gives Alamadiyaf al-Masiyyah "almost a month to progress its funding arrangements" as requested by Capricorn.
  • Company previously provided due diligence access earlier this year after evaluating multiple proposals.
  • Shares +3.2% to 325.0p.

time_period: Director-related dealing disclosed 8 April 2026
body:
• Rebecca Papp (person closely associated with Group Merchandise & Operations Director Richard Papp) sold 4,033 shares at GBP129.25.
  • Total value of sale: GBP521,265.
  • Reported current share price: 13,305.0p; 12-month change: +21%.

time_period: Q1 2026 trading update
body:
• Integrated Gas output expected at 880k–920k boepd in Q1 2026 vs 948k boepd in Q4 2025 (-7.2% to -3.0%).
  • Integrated Gas guidance lowered vs prior guidance of 920k–980k boepd (new high end 920k vs old high end 980k = -6.1%).
  • Shell attributed lower Integrated Gas output to Middle East conflict impacts on Qatari volumes.
  • LNG volumes guidance raised at the low end to 7.6–8.0 mt from 7.4–8.0 mt (+2.7% on the low end).
  • LNG drivers: "ramp-up of LNG Canada" offset by Australia weather constraints and Qatar LNG outages.
  • Shell warning: "in light of the ongoing situation in the Middle East, the outlook provided is subject to increased uncertainty."
  • Non-cash net-debt expected to be impacted by a USD3–4bn increase in "variable components of long-term shipping leases".
  • Working capital expected to swing to negative USD10–15bn in Q1 2026 vs positive USD1.3bn in Q4 2025, citing "unprecedented volatility in commodity prices on inventory and receivables."
  • Upstream production expected at 1.76–1.86m boepd vs 1.89m boepd in Q4 2025 (-6.9% to -1.6%).
  • Chemicals & Products indicative refining margin: USD17/bbl vs USD14/bbl in Q4 (+21.4%).
  • Trading & optimisation expected to be "significantly higher" than Q4.
  • Market context: Brent crude at USD93.76 vs USD110.24 at prior close (-15.0%) after US–Iran ceasefire; Shell shares -5.3% to 3,377.7p.

time_period: FY ended 31 March 2026 (FY26)
body:
• FY26 retail volumes: 65,000 vehicles, a record, +8.0% vs FY25.
  • Calendar 2025 retail sales volumes +6.0%, outperforming used car market growth of +2.2% (+3.8pp).
  • AI initiative: "Since launching our new agentic AI tool to reactivate historic closed quotes, around 900 incremental vehicle sales can be attributed to this channel."
  • Vehicle supply "returned to more normalised levels", enabling more bulk deals.
  • Record metal margin performance, supported by data-driven buying and pricing.
  • Expecting FY26 pretax profit of ~GBP7.5m, up 83% from GBP4.1m FY25.
  • New store planned: Leeds opening during the summer.
  • Annual results expected in June.
  • Shares +4.6% to 136.0p.

time_period: FCA motor finance redress scheme update (April 2026) + restructuring and HY results context
body:
• Estimates total cost of FCA's final motor finance redress scheme at ~GBP320m, "broadly similar" to existing IAS 37 provision of GBP294m (+8.8%).
  • Costs can be "comfortably absorbed" by existing capital resources.
  • "At this stage, no provision changes have been recognised. The group's existing IAS 37 provision remains under review."
  • Capital impact: ~25 bps CET1 reduction to 14.0% pro forma; still above target 12%–13%.
  • FCA estimates ~12.1m agreements eligible, down from 14.2m (-14.8%). Compensation ~GBP7.5bn; total incl. admin ~GBP9.1bn.
  • UBS: outcome "reduces potential downside risks". Sensitivity: 75% take-up assumed; each 5% change implies ~GBP18m (~17 bps CET1).
  • Panmure Liberum: ability to navigate redress without a fundraise; conservative scenario ~GBP426m still absorbable.
  • Shore Capital: downgraded to hold, "Take profits" — "underlying operational performance remains weak".
  • Share move: +16% to 451.54p (best FTSE 250 performer that morning).
  • Restructuring: workforce reduction ~20% by end FY27; savings target GBP25m this year, GBP60m by end next FY.
  • H1 results: pretax loss GBP65.5m vs GBP102.2m prior; operating income -6.1% to GBP333.8m; NIM 7.1% vs 7.3%.

time_period: Quarter ended March 2026 (FY2026 Q3) and FY2026 year-to-date
body:
• Q3 production: 82,723 oz gold and 4,128 tonnes copper.
  • FY2026 YTD production: 249,887 oz gold and 11.022 tonnes copper.
  • Sales exceeded production: 97,800 oz gold sold (+18.2% vs produced); 4,620 t copper sold (+11.9%).
  • Cash generation: ~USD260m in the quarter after capex and USD73m tax payment.
  • Cash balance USD1.21bn at 31 March, no debt, up from USD948m at end-2025 (+27.6%).
  • Guidance: expects full-year production "around or slightly above the upper end" of 260k–310k oz gold range.
  • Telfer project not impacted by diesel supply disruptions; plant largely powered by natural gas.
  • Shares +14% to 762.5p.

time_period: Refinancing / consent solicitation (consents as of 7 April 2026; process through 24 April 2026)
body:
• Consent solicitation for $1,285,245,000 10.25% Senior Secured Notes due 2026: >97% of principal consented as of 7 April 2026.
  • Required ≥90% threshold achieved.
  • Consents approve indenture/intercreditor amendments, issuance of New Notes, and release of collateral.
  • Consent solicitation remains open until 11:59 p.m. (NYC time) 21 April 2026.
  • 23 April 2026: portion of existing notes redeemed at par, pro rata, plus accrued interest.
  • 24 April 2026: holders receive New Notes, accrued interest, and a 1.00% fee.
  • All holders bound "whether or not such Holder delivered a consent or participated."

time_period: FCA motor finance redress scheme update (April 2026) — shorter item
body:
• Reiterates expected FCA redress cost of ~GBP320m, "broadly similar" to GBP294m IAS 37 provision (+8.8%).
  • Cost "comfortably absorbed" by existing capital.
  • No provision changes recognised; remains under review.
  • ~25 bps CET1 reduction to 14.0% pro forma, above target 12%–13%.
  • FCA scheme: 12.1m eligible agreements vs 14.2m proposed (-14.8%); compensation ~GBP7.5bn; total ~GBP9.1bn.
  • Shares +18% to 457.94p.

Time Period FCA motor finance redress scheme impact review (pro forma as at 31 January 2026; rollout summer 2026–end 2027)

• Reviewed FCA Policy Statement PS26/3 (motor finance consumer redress scheme) and its impact; stated the published scheme’s estimated cost is c.£320m, “broadly similar” to existing provision and “can be comfortably absorbed” by existing capital resources.

• No provision changes recognised yet; existing IAS 37 provision remains under review.

• Estimated scheme cost (if recognised in isolation) implies pro forma provision c.£320m as at 31 Jan 2026 vs IAS 37 provision £294m (difference +£26m / +8.8%).

• Capital impact: would reduce CET1 ratio by c.25 bps to 14.0% (pro forma at 31 Jan 2026), still above medium-term target 12–13%.

• Provision methodology: current £294m based on probability-weighted scenarios (including an implementation scenario aligned to the FCA’s 7 Oct 2025 consultation); the £320m estimate reflects the policy statement scenario only and changes relative to the consultation.

• Key assumption: c.720k qualifying UK regulated motor finance loans written 6 Apr 2007–1 Nov 2024, including c.640k DCA loans + c.80k non-DCA loans likely meeting “tied” and/or “high commission” criteria.

• Key assumption: average redress payment c.£500 incl. compensatory interest (vs industry average £829 cited by FCA), reflecting smaller loan sizes and lower commission levels in Close Brothers’ book.

• Key assumption: 75% claim rate; each ±5% absolute change in claim rate drives ±c.£18m change in estimate (incl. delivery cost impact).

• Key assumption: delivery cost c.£66m, excluding c.£14m of costs already incurred against the existing provision.

• Key assumption: scheme rollout from summer 2026 to end-CY 2027, implying a time-value discount of c.£16m.

• Footnote mechanics: the £294m balance sheet provision at 31 Jan 2026 reflects £300m charged to income statement, plus £8m partial unwind of discount (time value), less £14m costs incurred to date.

time_period: Order delivery through May 2028; revenue recognition from H2 2026
body:
• Won multiple orders for its titanium stress joint (TSJ) product line for a new offshore development in Guyana.
  • Award value: USD63.5 million.
  • Delivery schedule: through May 2028.
  • Revenue recognition expected to start in H2 2026.
  • Work will be completed by the Subsea Spring business unit.
  • These awards are in addition to USD4.4 million of orders already secured for the same development by Hunting's Stafford and Flexible Engineered Solutions business units since end-2025.
  • Hunting expects further incremental orders for this Guyana development "throughout the remainder of 2026".
  • Hunting frames the awards as validating its TSJ solution on FPSO developments, citing "maintenance benefits and reliability".
  • CEO Jim Johnson said: "Our TSJ product line, which is a critical component for offshore developments, continues to be adopted on key offshore projects utilising FPSOs, given the maintenance benefits and reliability offered."
  • CEO Jim Johnson said: "This order also contributes to our guided subsea product group revenue and [earnings before interest, tax, depreciation, and amortisation] through to 2028, as published at our subsea investor event held in January 2026."

time_period: Deep level drilling programme (March–December 2025); resource/reserve update expected during 2026
body:
• Reported better-than-expected results from its deep level drilling programme at the Blanket mine (Zimbabwe).
  • Stated the orebodies returned grades and widths consistent with or exceeding expectations, prompting an intent to update the mineral resource estimate during 2026.
  • Said the results suggest gold mineralisation may extend deeper than previously anticipated.
  • Market reaction noted: shares up 5.7% to 1,850p (Tuesday morning, London).
  • Completed 10,312 metres of deep level drilling between March and December 2025.
  • Eroica orebody (≈1 km north of Blanket): results consistent with expectations.
  • Blanket orebody: identified a new intercept with "excellent" grades up to 9.2 g/t at 1,045m depth.
  • Newly discovered orebody has been named "Blanket 7".
  • Lima orebody: confirmed to "34 level", meaning gold present at 1,110m depth; Caledonia says this supports potential for further depth extensions.
  • Said deep drilling results will be incorporated into an updated mineral resource and mineral reserve statement to be released this year.
  • CEO said: "The latest results from our deep drilling programme reinforce the geological strength of Blanket mine and demonstrate the continuity of mineralisation at depth across multiple orebodies. The consistency of grades and widths we are seeing...provides growing confidence in the scale and quality of the mineral resource below the current lowest levels of the mine."
  • CEO said: "These outcomes support our longer term planning efforts and highlight the value of the investments we have made".

time_period: Share buyback programme from 07 April 2026 to on/before 31 December 2026
body:
• Announced commencement of a third share buyback programme (the "2026 Programme") following its FY results release on 17 March 2026.
  • Maximum aggregate market value to be bought back: £10,000,000 (excluding associated costs and stamp duty).
  • Programme start date: 07 April 2026.
  • Programme end date: on or before 31 December 2026.
  • Stated sole purpose: reduce share capital.
  • Company intends to cancel any shares purchased.
  • Entered into non-discretionary instructions with Investec Bank plc to conduct the programme.
  • Investec will act as principal and make trading decisions independently of Wickes.
  • Programme is within existing AGM authority, which at the date of announcement equals 16,502,960 ordinary shares.
  • Said the maximum aggregate number of shares purchasable will be updated to reflect authority granted at the 2026 AGM (19 May 2026).
  • Programme to be conducted in accordance with Market Abuse Regulation (596/2014) (as retained in UK law) and the FCA Listing Rules.

time_period: Director dealing disclosures covering purchases on 07 April 2026 and 01 April 2026
body:
• Executive Chair Robert Perrins bought 7,000 shares at £31.79 each (total £222,543) in London on 07 April 2026.
  • This follows a prior purchase of 7,000 shares at £28.78 each (total £201,482) on 01 April 2026.
  • The later purchase price (£31.79) is £3.01 higher than the earlier purchase price (£28.78), a +10.5% increase.
  • Combined across the two disclosed purchases: 14,000 shares acquired for £424,025 (total consideration).
  • The purchases were reported as occurring after Berkeley shares fell following the company's decision to halt land buying, framed as linked to a UK housing market downturn attributed to the conflict in the Middle East.

time_period: Q1 2026 outlook and FY 2026 forecast revisions
body:
• Broker Peel Hunt moved its rating to "reduce" (sell) and cut its price target to 3,155p from 3,285p (-4.0%).
  • Peel Hunt relayed management's view that Q1 is likely to be the "softest" quarter for production in 2026.
  • Peel Hunt reduced its Q1 copper production forecast to 136,000 tonnes.
  • Peel Hunt forecasts Q1 production down 12% year-on-year.
  • Peel Hunt forecasts Q1 production down 23% quarter-on-quarter.
  • Peel Hunt said the "net effect" is a 29,000 tonne reduction to its 2026 production forecast, now 642,000 tonnes.
  • Peel Hunt cut its FY 2026 revenue estimate by 5% to USD8.8 billion.
  • Peel Hunt cut its 2026 adjusted EBITDA estimate by 8% to USD5.3 billion.
  • Peel Hunt said: "the 'lower output impacts revenue directly'".
  • Peel Hunt said its base case implies the shares trade at "early 10x 2026 adjusted Ebitda", which it described as "more typical of trough-cycle valuations rather than a period of record copper prices."
  • Peel Hunt warned: "Another year of copper output near the bottom of the guidance range, or a further cut to guidance, could continue to test investor patience and result in further share price underperformance."

time_period: Early redemption on 20 May 2026; instrument originally due May 2027
body:
• Announced notice of redemption for A$250,000,000 Floating Rate Debt Instruments due May 2027 (ISIN: AU3FN0060448).
  • Will fully redeem all outstanding instruments on the interest payment date falling on or immediately after 20 May 2026 (the "Early Redemption Date (Call)").
  • Redemption price: 100% of principal plus accrued but unpaid interest.
  • Accrued interest period specified: from (and including) 20 February 2026 to (but excluding) 20 May 2026.
  • Payment date for the redemption amount: 20 May 2026.
  • Stated that the LSE International Securities Market listing of the instruments will be cancelled on, or shortly after, 21 May 2026.
  • Redemption is stated to be in accordance with Condition 10.4 of the instruments' terms and conditions.
  • Referenced documentation: the company's Supplement dated 18 May 2021 and Information Memorandum dated 7 May 2021.
  • Provided contact for queries: Barclays Debt Investor Relations, +44 20 7116 1000, 1 Churchill Place, London E14 5HP.

time_period: Share repurchase executed 02 April 2026 (announced 07 April 2026)
body:
• Acquired 80,000 ordinary shares on 02 April 2026 at an average price of 92.88 pence per share.
  • Shares purchased were placed into Treasury.
  • Implied cash consideration for the shares (excluding fees/expenses) is ~GBP74,304.
  • Issued share capital stated as 271,661,725 ordinary shares.
  • Total treasury shares following the purchase: 12,787,534.
  • Treasury shares are ~4.71% of issued share capital.
  • Total shares with voting rights following the purchase: 258,874,191.

time_period: Board changes effective 07 April 2026 (following GM on 12 March 2026)
body:
• Following shareholder approval at the General Meeting on 12 March 2026, Leo Quinn assumes the role of Executive Chair with effect from 07 April 2026.
  • Andrew Harrison steps down from the Board with immediate effect.
  • Andrew Harrison resumes his role as CEO of the Group's UK division.
  • Leo Quinn is described as the former CEO of Balfour Beatty PLC.

time_period: Deep level drilling completed March–December 2025; targets resource confidence/extension beyond 34 level (1,110m depth)
body:
• Reports "better than expected grades and widths" from deep level drilling at Blanket Mine, supporting increased confidence in the overall mineral resource.
  • Deep drilling campaign completed 10,311.9 metres between March and December 2025.
  • Drilling objective: assess continuity of mineralised zones on the Blanket, Eroica and Lima orebodies to increase confidence in existing mineral resources.
  • Targeted growing the mineral resource estimate beyond 34 level, stated as 1,110 metres below surface.
  • States Blanket and Eroica orebodies returned grades/widths consistent with, or better than, expectations.
  • Notes multiple wide zones within newly identified Blanket 7 (BLK7) orebody as part of the "high grade intersections at depth" theme.
  • Says Lima orebody confirmed to 34 level, supporting potential depth extensions.
  • Expects results to increase mineral resource confidence and support future life-of-mine planning.
  • BLK7 (ARS1110EX2401): 13.02m true width at 9.24 g/t (intersection depth from surface 1045.3m).
  • BLK7 (ARS1110EX2402): 40.42m true width at 3.14 g/t (depth 1075.0m).
  • BLK7 (ARS1110EX2402, included interval): 6.38m true width at 9.90 g/t.
  • BLK7 (ARS1110EX2403): 46.28m true width at 4.25 g/t (depth 1082.0m).
  • BLK7 (ARS1110EX2403, included interval): 1.16m true width at 53.39 g/t.
  • BLK7 (ARS1110EX2404): 20.50m true width at 3.98 g/t (depth 1054.1m).
  • BQRHWN (ARS1110EX2503): 3.65m true width at 7.74 g/t (depth 1099.3m).
  • BQR (BLK1110EX2412): 7.17m true width at 7.15 g/t (depth 1083.0m).
  • ERCN_HW (ERC750EX2404): 10.80m true width at 6.54 g/t (depth 994.3m).
  • ERCN_HW (ERC750EX2404, included interval): 1.54m true width at 14.20 g/t.
  • LIMM (LIM750EX2509): 1.98m true width at 15.33 g/t (depth 708.5m).

time_period: March 2026 and Q1 2026 home closings (as at 31 March 2026)
body:
• March 2026 closings: 451 homes, including 9 currently/previously leased single-family rental homes (~2.0% of March closings).
  • Q1 2026 closings: 916 homes, including 35 currently/previously leased single-family rental homes (~3.8% of Q1 closings).
  • Active selling communities: 142 as of 31 March 2026 (vs 140 at 28 Feb 2026, i.e. +2).
  • Q1 2026 results: to be released before market open on Tuesday 28 April 2026, with a conference call at 12:30pm ET (17:30 BST).

time_period: Management appointments effective 02 April 2026; strategy work referenced "beyond" 2027 targets
body:
• Appointed Carol Howle as Deputy Chief Executive Officer, effective 02 April 2026.
  • Howle will continue to lead Supply, Trading & Shipping.
  • In the new role, Howle will oversee BP's ongoing portfolio review and strategic development.
  • BP notes Howle has spent 25 years with the company.
  • BP says Howle served as interim CEO in December 2025.
  • Meg O'Neill (described as CEO since 01 April 2026) said: "As I step into my new role I am committed to providing clear direction and consistency so our company can move forward with confidence."
  • Meg O'Neill said: "To support this, Carol will oversee the ongoing portfolio review and long-term strategy development beyond our 2027 targets. I'm looking forward to working closely with her to deliver a simpler, stronger, more valuable BP to maximize value for our shareholders."

time_period: Motor finance redress scheme update (as at 2 April 2026)
body:
• Provision for the motor finance issue remains GBP1.95bn; Lloyds said it has "undertaken an assessment of the implications and impact of the final rules" and that "the group does not currently believe any change to the provision for this issue is required."
  • FCA confirmed the industry-wide motor finance redress scheme on 30 March 2026.
  • FCA eligibility estimate tightened to 12.1m agreements (from 14.2m at consultation; -14.8%).
  • FCA expected total compensation ("redress") GBP7.5bn (from GBP8.2bn at consultation; -8.5%) and total industry cost (incl. admin) GBP9.1bn (from GBP11.0bn; -17.3%).
  • FCA says the scheme is being streamlined (incl. not requiring recorded delivery), claiming it cuts delivery cost to firms by >40%; also notes "in around 1 in 3 cases compensation will be capped."
  • Lloyds flagged residual uncertainty (response rates, litigation, operational costs): "The ultimate outcome may also differ dependent upon potential actions by various parties, including legal proceedings and complaints. An update will be provided as appropriate with the first quarter results at the end of April."
  • Lloyds Q1 results date: 29 April 2026.
  • Share move on the day: -1.6% to 96.18p, but up >5% over the week.
  • Shore Capital view: stance "appears reasonable" given FCA tweaks, but the key risks remain litigation and take-up; Shore also highlights the FCA's assumed participation rate moving to 75% from 85% (-11.8%) and estimates expected remediation costs for banks down to ~GBP5.2bn from ~GBP5.6bn.
  • External/legal overhang cited in press: Black Horse exposure includes a reported lawsuit involving ~30,000 customers seeking GBP66m.

time_period: March 2026 traffic statistics (and rolling 12 months to end-March 2026)
body:
• March passengers 15.8m (+5.3% YoY); load factor 93% (flat YoY).
  • March flights operated: 88,000+.
  • Rolling 12-month passengers 208.4m (+4.1% YoY); load factor 94% (flat YoY).
  • O'Leary on timing/risk of supply disruption: "But if the war continues, we do run the risk of supply disruptions in Europe in May and June and obviously we hope the war will finish sooner than that and that the risk to supply will be eliminated."
  • O'Leary on scenario outcomes: "It's almost impossible to know. If the war finishes and the Strait of Hormuz is opened by the middle or end of April, then there's no risk to supply."
  • O'Leary on potential scale of impact: "If the war continues, and the disruption to supply continues, we think there is a reasonable risk that maybe 10%, 20%, 25% of our supplies might be at risk through May and June."
  • O'Leary concluding: "So, like everybody else in the industry, we hope this war ends sooner rather than later."
  • Shares (early Thursday): -1.6% to EUR24.56.
  • Additional context from Reuters: Ryanair has warned prolonged conflict could force summer capacity reductions/cancellations if fuel availability tightens; it has also pointed to hedging and monitoring suppliers closely.

time_period: March 2026 traffic and CO₂ emission statistics (and rolling 12 months to end-March 2026)
body:
• March passengers 5.5m (+8.4% YoY).
  • March capacity +9.1% YoY to 6.12m seats.
  • March load factor 90.0% (from 90.5%; -0.5ppts YoY).
  • Rolling 12-month passengers 69.7m (+10% YoY).
  • Rolling 12-month load factor 90.7% (from 91.2%; -0.5ppts YoY).
  • CO₂ emissions per passenger-km: -2.7% YoY in March; -3.1% on a rolling 12-month basis.
  • Network/ops: continued expansion in Italy (adding aircraft for Milan/Rome/Venice) and opened a base at Podgorica (Montenegro).
  • Wizz made no comment on fuel supply risk in this update.
  • Shares (early Thursday): -4.0% to 866.50p.

time_period: Director dealings (shares bought on 1 April 2026; reported 2 April 2026)
body:
• Jane Eady (wife of CFO Neil Eady) bought 1,750 shares at GBP28.86 (value GBP50,497).
  • Executive Chair Robert Perrins bought 7,000 shares at GBP28.78 (value GBP201,482).
  • Share weakness catalyst cited: Berkeley said it has decided to halt land buying amid a UK housing market downturn it linked to the Middle East conflict; it also cited an "unprecedented" increase in UK cost/regulation.
  • Reported reference prices: 3,134.00p (+1.0% on the Thursday); 12-month change -12%.

time_period: Broker note / forward outlook (2026–2027)
body:
• Peel Hunt downgraded to 'hold' from 'buy', with price target unchanged at 270p.
  • Broker rationale: upgrade to medium-term production outlook (stable 2026; +3% growth in 2027 vs prior), but rating cut after a sharp share-price move.
  • Related company context: Harbour reported record 2025 production of 474 kboepd (+84% YoY) and refreshed its shareholder returns framework (linking returns to free cash flow/leverage).

time_period: Board change effective 31 May 2026
body:
• Barbara Moorhouse will step down as a Non-executive Director on 31 May 2026 after nine years on the board.
  • Chair quote (Charles Allen, Lord Allen of Kensington, CBE): "On behalf of the Board, I would like to thank Barbara for her outstanding contribution to Balfour Beatty over the past nine years. Barbara has brought great insight, rigour and sound judgement to her role, and her service on the Audit and Risk, Remuneration and Nomination Committees has been highly valued. We are grateful for her significant commitment and dedication, and we wish her every success in the future."

time_period: Interim CFO departure (effective 2 April 2026) and leaver terms (FY ending June 2026)
body:
• Marie Wall stepped down from the Board and as Interim CFO on 2 April 2026 (following the 20 Jan 2026 announcement).
  • Marie received salary, pension and benefits up to 2 April 2026, plus a payment in lieu of accrued but untaken holiday.
  • Annual bonus: treated as a good leaver; eligible for an annual pro-rata bonus for the financial year ending June 2026, subject to Remuneration Committee approval, paid at the normal payment date.
  • Share plan: awards under the DFS Group Share Plan lapse on leaving; includes a grant of 182,724 shares.
  • Disclosure: remuneration details to be included in the Directors' Remuneration Report in the Annual Report for the year ending 28 June 2026.
